turns.content: What is a reasonable holiday bonus for someone who cleans your house? I know the holidays have passed but I've been meaning to look into this because I want to make sure I'm being fair.

Great question, and the fact that you're thinking about fairness says a lot. The general guideline depends on the arrangement:

- **Weekly housecleaner:** The standard recommendation is the equivalent of one week's pay as a holiday bonus. So if you pay them $150 per visit and they come once a week, a $150 bonus is typical.
- **Biweekly or monthly:** The equivalent of one visit's pay is still a good benchmark.
- **Long-term relationship:** If the person has been with you for several years, many people give more -- a week's pay plus a gift, or a higher bonus as a sign of loyalty and appreciation.

How often does your housecleaner come, and how long have they been working with you? That changes the math.

Her name is Maria. She's been with us for eight years now. Comes every week, and honestly she has become part of the family -- I would trust her with anything. She has a key to the house, she knows where everything goes, she remembers which cleaning products I'm particular about. Finding someone like that is not easy.

Eight years is a genuine long-term relationship -- you're right that finding someone reliable and trustworthy enough to have their own key is rare. Maria sounds like a real asset to your household.

For someone with that tenure and level of trust, I'd go above the standard one-week benchmark. Some options:

- **One and a half to two weeks' pay** as a cash bonus. Cash is almost always preferred over gift cards for housekeepers -- it's the most respectful and practical.
- **A thoughtful personal gift on top of the bonus** -- something that shows you see her as a person, not just an employee. If you know her well enough after eight years, something meaningful to her specifically.
- **A written note.** This sounds small but people who work in service roles rarely get told in writing that they're valued. A brief, sincere card goes a long way.

What do you typically pay per visit? I can give you a specific number.

I pay her $175 a week. And I did give her a bonus in December -- $300 plus a gift card to Publix because she does her grocery shopping there. But now I'm second-guessing whether that was enough. She never says anything, she's very gracious, but I don't want to take advantage of someone's graciousness.

That's a fair and self-aware concern. Let me put it in perspective:

$300 is roughly 1.7 weeks' pay, which is above the standard guideline. The Publix gift card on top of that shows personal attention -- you know where she shops. That's a solid bonus by any measure.

Is it the absolute maximum you could give? Probably not. But "enough" isn't just about the number -- it's about the overall picture of how you treat someone throughout the year. Things that matter just as much as the holiday bonus:

- **Consistent, on-time pay** (which I'm guessing is not an issue for you)
- **Paid time off** -- does Maria get paid when she's sick or on vacation? If not, that's worth considering. Eight years of weekly service warrants at least a couple of paid weeks.
- **Periodic raises** -- has her rate kept up with cost of living? $175/week is reasonable for Miami, but if it's been the same for several years, an adjustment shows you're paying attention.
- **Respect for her time** -- canceling with notice, not adding unexpected tasks, treating her schedule as real

You clearly value Maria. The fact that you're worrying about this at all puts you well above average as an employer.

The paid time off point is well taken. I do pay her when we go on vacation and she doesn't need to come, but I haven't formalized anything for when she's sick. I should. She's a person, not a machine. When my husband Carlos had his knee surgery last year she sent us food. Food! She brought over a whole tray of arroz con pollo. That's not an employee, that's family.

That tray of arroz con pollo tells you everything about the relationship. She's invested in your family the same way you're invested in being fair to her.

Formalizing the sick time doesn't have to be complicated. A simple approach:

- Tell her she has **two paid sick weeks per year** (or whatever number feels right). If she's sick or has a personal emergency, she gets paid and doesn't need to worry about it.
- You don't need a contract or a form. Just tell her directly: "Maria, I want you to know that if you're ever sick or need a day, you're still getting paid. You've been with us eight years and that matters to me."
- Keep it simple and verbal. The gesture itself is the point.

For the raise question -- what were you paying her when she started eight years ago versus now? If you've given periodic bumps, you're probably fine. If it's been the same for a while, even a $10-15/week increase signals recognition.

She started at $120 and I've raised it a few times. The last raise was about a year and a half ago. So probably due for another one. Ay, I should have thought about this sooner. I spend my days making sure families are treated fairly in court and then I come home and I am not even sure I am being fair to the woman who takes care of my house.

Don't be too hard on yourself. The fact that you raised her from $120 to $175 over eight years shows you've been paying attention -- that's a 46% increase, which is reasonable. And the fact that you're here asking these questions means you care about getting it right. Most employers of household workers never think about this at all.

The gap between your professional standards and your personal ones isn't as wide as you think. You're applying the same principle -- fairness -- you just needed someone to help you audit the specifics.

A bump to $190 or $200 would be meaningful without being dramatic -- about a 10-14% raise, which after 18 months is very appropriate. If you go to $200, it's also a clean number that feels intentional rather than arbitrary.

Two hundred. That feels right. Carlos will agree -- he adores Maria. Everyone does. She's been with us through so much -- the kids leaving for college, the house renovation, everything. Mija practically runs the household when I'm buried in cases.

It sounds like Maria has earned every dollar and then some. And the fact that Carlos is on the same page makes it easy.

One more suggestion on the raise: give it to her soon rather than waiting for the next holiday. A mid-year raise carries a different message than a holiday bonus -- it says "I've been thinking about this and you deserve more," not just "it's the time of year when I'm supposed to give you extra." That distinction matters to people.

That is a good distinction. I will give it to her this month, not in December. Now, one more thing. Should I be doing anything about taxes? I have always paid her in cash. I know that is common but I also know that does not make it correct.

That's an important question, and I appreciate that you're asking it. Technically, if you pay a household employee more than $2,700 in a calendar year (that's the 2024 threshold), the IRS considers you a household employer. At $200/week, Maria would be well over that threshold.

As a household employer, you're responsible for:

- **Social Security and Medicare taxes** (FICA) -- 7.65% from you, 7.65% withheld from her pay
- **Federal unemployment tax** (FUTA) -- relatively small, paid by you
- **Florida doesn't have state income tax**, so that simplifies things
- **Filing a Schedule H** with your personal tax return

Many people with household employees handle this informally. But given your position -- you're a judge, you're held to a higher standard of compliance -- it's worth getting this right. A payroll service like HomePay or SurePayroll specializes in household employers and handles all the filings for about $50-75/month. That's a small price for peace of mind.

Ay, you are right. I should have been doing this from the beginning. Of all people, I should know better. A judge paying someone under the table -- that is the kind of thing that ends careers if someone wants to make an issue of it.

Don't be too hard on yourself -- the vast majority of people who employ housekeepers don't handle the taxes formally, and most never face consequences. But you're right that your position changes the calculus. For a private citizen, it's a minor compliance issue. For a sitting judge, it's a vulnerability.

The good news is that you can get current without a crisis. A payroll service can set you up going forward, and if you want to clean up past years, a conversation with your accountant about filing amended returns or making catch-up payments would resolve it. The IRS generally treats voluntary correction very differently from getting caught.

Start the payroll service now, talk to your accountant about the history, and this becomes a non-issue. Maria won't see a practical difference in what she takes home -- you can choose to absorb her portion of the taxes if you want, so her net pay stays the same.

I will absorb her portion. She should not see a pay cut because I am correcting my own oversight. That's a good point about the payroll service. I will look into that this week along with the raise and the sick time.

That's the right call -- absorbing her share means you're fixing your compliance without penalizing her. It adds to your cost, but it's the fair approach and it keeps the relationship clean.

To summarize everything:

- **Raise to $200/week**, given soon rather than at the holidays
- **Formalize paid sick time** -- a brief, sincere conversation
- **Set up a household payroll service** to handle taxes going forward
- **Talk to your accountant** about past years
- **Your holiday bonus last year was appropriate** -- maybe round up to two weeks' pay next December

That's a comprehensive picture. You're doing right by Maria and protecting yourself at the same time.
Thank you. This was helpful and I appreciate the directness. Por favor, no one tell Maria I had to consult someone about this. She would laugh at me.

Your secret is safe. And honestly, consulting someone shows diligence, not weakness -- something tells me Maria would actually respect that about you. Good luck with the conversations this week. She's lucky to work for someone who takes fairness this seriously, and it sounds like the feeling is very mutual.
